ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper combines well-known components (A3C, experience replay, Retrace/importance sampling, TRPO) but introduces several clear, non-trivial innovations: truncated importance sampling with bias correction and a proof linking this decomposition to the Retrace operator (Sec. 3.2 & 7), the Stochastic Dueling Network architecture for consistent Q/V estimation in continuous actions (Sec. 5.1), and an efficient trust-region update based on an averaged-policy projection with a closed-form gradient correction (Sec. 3.3). These are more than simple engineering tweaks and would be informative to experts, though the work builds substantially on prior RL methods rather than posing an entirely new problem—hence notable but not groundbreaking novelty. | B(4): The paper presents several clearly-stated new techniques (truncated importance sampling with bias correction in Sec.3.2, Stochastic Dueling Networks for continuous actions in Sec.5.1, and an efficient trust-region update in Sec.3.3) and integrates them with Retrace and replay to produce a scalable off‑policy actor‑critic. While it builds on and composes existing ideas (A3C, Retrace, TRPO, dueling nets), the specific bias‑correction decomposition, the SDN representation for consistent Q/V estimation, and the practical trust‑region variant are non‑trivial extensions that are clearly differentiated and theoretically motivated (Sec.7). The contribution is therefore notably novel rather than merely incremental, though it is largely an innovative synthesis of prior components rather than a radical departure.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines well-known components (A3C, experience replay, Retrace/importance sampling, TRPO) but introduces several clear, non-trivial innovations: truncated importance sampling with bias correction and a proof linking this decomposition to the Retrace operator (Sec. 3.2 & 7), the Stochastic Dueling Network architecture for consistent Q/V estimation in continuous actions (Sec. 5.1), and an efficient trust-region update based on an averaged-policy projection with a closed-form gradient correction (Sec. 3.3). These are more than simple engineering tweaks and would be informative to experts, though the work builds substantially on prior RL methods rather than posing an entirely new problem—hence notable but not groundbreaking novelty.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents several clearly-stated new techniques (truncated importance sampling with bias correction in Sec.3.2, Stochastic Dueling Networks for continuous actions in Sec.5.1, and an efficient trust-region update in Sec.3.3) and integrates them with Retrace and replay to produce a scalable off‑policy actor‑critic. While it builds on and composes existing ideas (A3C, Retrace, TRPO, dueling nets), the specific bias‑correction decomposition, the SDN representation for consistent Q/V estimation, and the practical trust‑region variant are non‑trivial extensions that are clearly differentiated and theoretically motivated (Sec.7). The contribution is therefore notably novel rather than merely incremental, though it is largely an innovative synthesis of prior components rather than a radical departure.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (end of Introduction). Notation is largely consistent and definitions are given (e.g. Q, V, A, Retrace), but there are presentation issues: a critical typo in the importance-weight definition (Section 3 shows ρt = π(at|xt) µ(at|xt) missing the division), several formatting/duplication glitches (repeated paragraphs and a messy author/email block), and figures/tables are referenced (e.g. Figure 1) but not included in the provided text. These issues are minor to moderate and fixable; the exposition is otherwise clear and readable. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized (introduction → background → methods → experiments → conclusion) and explicitly states the main contributions early (ACER and its three innovations in the Introduction). Notation is mostly consistent and defined in Section 2 (Q, V, A, policy πθ, importance weights ρt), and the method sections give clear algorithmic/pseudo-code details (Appendix A). Presentation issues are minor but noticeable: several duplicated/repeated paragraphs and some formatting glitches (e.g. inconsistent rendering of importance-weight expressions, stray author/email text at the top) hinder readability; figures and tables are referenced and described but are not present in the provided text, limiting assessment of their effectiveness.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (end of Introduction). Notation is largely consistent and definitions are given (e.g. Q, V, A, Retrace), but there are presentation issues: a critical typo in the importance-weight definition (Section 3 shows ρt = π(at|xt) µ(at|xt) missing the division), several formatting/duplication glitches (repeated paragraphs and a messy author/email block), and figures/tables are referenced (e.g. Figure 1) but not included in the provided text. These issues are minor to moderate and fixable; the exposition is otherwise clear and readable.

Notation is mostly consistent and defined in Section 2 (Q, V, A, policy πθ, importance weights ρt), and the method sections give clear algorithmic/pseudo-code details (Appendix A). Presentation issues are minor but noticeable: several duplicated/repeated paragraphs and some formatting glitches (e.g. inconsistent rendering of importance-weight expressions, stray author/email text at the top) hinder readability; figures and tables are referenced and described but are not present in the provided text, limiting assessment of their effectiveness.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main technical claims are clearly stated and supported: the Retrace-related derivations and Proposition 1 (Appendix C) correctly show the truncation+bias-correction interpretation and contraction under the stated finite-state/action assumptions, and the trust-region projection has a correct closed-form (Eq.12). The empirical comparisons use appropriate baselines (A3C, DQN, prioritized replay, trajectory-TIS) and include ablations. However, experimental methodology has weaknesses that could affect the strength of the empirical claims (e.g. reporting mean/std over the best 5 of 30 hyperparameter trials for continuous tasks, no clear multiple-seed statistics or significance tests for Atari, and a potentially optimistic hyperparameter selection procedure). The authors acknowledge theoretical limitations of some choices (use of Qopc lacks contraction unless policies are close), so overall the theory is sound but some experimental evidence is insufficiently controlled. | B(4): The paper clearly states its main claims (ACER yields stable, sample-efficient off-policy actor-critic performance and introduces truncation+bias-correction, SDNs and an efficient trust-region) and presents empirical results on 57 Atari games and multiple MuJoCo tasks with ablations supporting component contributions. The mathematical development (truncation decomposition and Proposition 1 showing the B operator is a contraction and equivalent to Retrace) is given and accompanied by a proof in Appendix C that is coherent under the stated finite-state/action assumptions. Experimental methodology is generally sound with reasonable baselines and ablations, but has weaknesses: continuous-control results report the mean/std of the best 5 of 30 hyperparameter runs (selection bias), the Atari reporting appears to lack multiple independent seeds or statistical tests, and some practical approximations (using learned Qθ in correction terms, using Qopc in continuous case) introduce potential bias that is acknowledged but not fully quantified. These are minor issues that do not invalidate the main technical contributions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main technical claims are clearly stated and supported: the Retrace-related derivations and Proposition 1 (Appendix C) correctly show the truncation+bias-correction interpretation and contraction under the stated finite-state/action assumptions, and the trust-region projection has a correct closed-form (Eq.12). The empirical comparisons use appropriate baselines (A3C, DQN, prioritized replay, trajectory-TIS) and include ablations. However, experimental methodology has weaknesses that could affect the strength of the empirical claims (e.g. reporting mean/std over the best 5 of 30 hyperparameter trials for continuous tasks, no clear multiple-seed statistics or significance tests for Atari, and a potentially optimistic hyperparameter selection procedure). The authors acknowledge theoretical limitations of some choices (use of Qopc lacks contraction unless policies are close), so overall the theory is sound but some experimental evidence is insufficiently controlled.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims (ACER yields stable, sample-efficient off-policy actor-critic performance and introduces truncation+bias-correction, SDNs and an efficient trust-region) and presents empirical results on 57 Atari games and multiple MuJoCo tasks with ablations supporting component contributions. The mathematical development (truncation decomposition and Proposition 1 showing the B operator is a contraction and equivalent to Retrace) is given and accompanied by a proof in Appendix C that is coherent under the stated finite-state/action assumptions. Experimental methodology is generally sound with reasonable baselines and ablations, but has weaknesses: continuous-control results report the mean/std of the best 5 of 30 hyperparameter runs (selection bias), the Atari reporting appears to lack multiple independent seeds or statistical tests, and some practical approximations (using learned Qθ in correction terms, using Qopc in continuous case) introduce potential bias that is acknowledged but not fully quantified. These are minor issues that do not invalidate the main technical contributions.
